title: Polylogarithmic Structure of Bragg Diffraction in Finite-Coherence Lattices
abstract: We develop a polylogarithmic structure for Bragg diffraction based on a weighted multi-plane interference model. Within this kind of construction, the scattering amplitude is expressed as a polylogarithmic generating function. By introducing extra contributions with power-law and the usual exponential decay, it takes the form $F(θ) = \mathrm{Li}_m\left(e^{iθ_{\mathrm{eff}} - ε}\right)$, where $ε$ is a finite coherence length. In the limit where $ε\rightarrow 0$, the argument of the polylogarithm approaches the unit circle and the classical Bragg condition corresponds to the approach of the polylogarithm argument toward its branch point $z=1$. This formulation provides a compact analytical framework for describing diffraction line shapes within a generalized correlation model in which peak positions, widths, and line shapes arise from a single analytic structure. Although we are able to recover the standard Bragg law for ideal crystals, the polylogarithm model captures deviations due to finite correlation length, disorder and non-uniform lattice coherence. We show that if Bragg peaks correspond to boundary singularities of the polylogarithm, a connection between diffraction theory and complex analysis arise. The proposed theoretical model may be particularly relevant for disordered or partially coherent materials, where conventional diffraction models often require additional phenomenological broadening assumptions.
summary: This paper introduces a polylogarithmic structure to model Bragg diffraction in materials with finite coherence or disorder. By framing the scattering amplitude as a function of a polylogarithm, it unifies peak positions, widths, and line shapes into one analytic description. This framework is particularly valuable for analyzing real-world disordered systems where conventional models require ad hoc broadening assumptions.
main_problem: To develop a generalized diffraction formalism that extends classical Bragg law by explicitly incorporating effects of finite coherence length, disorder, and non-uniform lattice weighting.
main_result: The polylogarithm model provides a compact analytical framework where peak positions, widths, and line shapes arise from a single analytic structure, connecting diffraction theory to complex analysis via boundary singularities.
method: The scattering amplitude is modeled using a weighted multi-plane interference approach, expressing it as a polylogarithmic generating function $\text{Li}_m(z)$.
model_or_system: The model analyzes Bragg diffraction from lattices with structural correlations, utilizing an effective Hamiltonian and pair correlation functions $C(r) \sim 1/r^m e^{-r/k_0}$.
key_observables: Scattering amplitude $F(\theta)$, measurable intensity $I(\theta)=|F(\theta)|^2$, peak positions, and line shapes.
important_parameters: Finite coherence length ($\epsilon=1/k_0$), algebraic suppression exponent ($m$), and the complex variable argument $z$.
main_assumptions: The model is intended to generalize Bragg law; it assumes a specific form for correlation decay and that peak singularities map onto polylogarithm branch points.
figures_summary: Figures show the polylogarithmic Bragg peak for $\text{TiO}_2$ at varying coherence lengths, confirming line shape modification without significant peak position shifts.
paper_structure: The paper develops the mathematical framework using polylogarithms to describe scattering amplitudes in correlated lattices. It analyzes the limit $k_0 \to \infty$ (ideal case) and then examines finite-$k_0$ deviations, drawing analogies between diffraction singularities and critical phenomena/hyperbolic geometry.
why_interesting: The connection drawn between lattice interference patterns and complex analysis (e.g., hyperbolic geometry via the dilogarithm) provides a powerful mathematical tool for analyzing quasi-periodic or disordered systems beyond standard scattering theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Polylogarithmic Bragg peak for TiO2 for various values of k0 parameter. We observe that finite reduced long-range order pro- duces negligible peak-position corrections but modifies the diffraction line shape and broadening.
